original_abstract:
Developing iron-based complexes as sustainable alternatives to rare-metal photosensitizers is a major challenge due to the rapid non-radiative deactivation of their charge-transfer states. While recent advances have achieved emissive iron compounds, dual luminescence from a single iron center has remained elusive. Here, we present the synthesis and characterization of a cyclometalated Fe(III) complex, [Fe(ImP)₂][PF₆], which exhibits a unique Janus-type dual emission from both ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT) and metal-to-ligand charge transfer (MLCT) states. 

The molecular design utilizes a pincer-type ligand framework that integrates four N-heterocyclic carbenes with two cyclometalating aryl units. This specific coordination environment stabilizes the Fe(IV) oxidation state and provides accessible low-lying $\pi^*$ orbitals, enabling an MLCT-dominated excited state at higher energy that does not relax into the lower-lying LMCT state. Ultrafast transient absorption and streak camera measurements reveal a remarkably long MLCT lifetime of 4.6 ns, currently the longest reported for a charge-transfer state in an iron complex. Furthermore, we demonstrate that this high-energy MLCT state possesses potent redox character, capable of undergoing both reductive and oxidative quenching. These findings expand the photophysical limits of earth-abundant metal complexes and establish a new structural paradigm for developing iron-based multifunctional photoredox catalysts and optical materials.
humanized_abstract:
Designing iron-based complexes as a sustainable alternative to rare-metal photosensitizers remains a formidable task due to the fast radiative decay of the charge-transfer states of these systems. This hurdle has only recently been partially overcome by achieving luminescence in iron-based systems, albeit not from the same iron atom at the same time. In this work, we report the design and characterization of a cyclometalated Fe(III) complex, [Fe(ImP)₂][PF₆], that is the first example of a Janus-type complex that features, to our knowledge, a rare dual-luminescence behavior originating from LMCT and MLCT states, respectively.

A new molecular design based on a pincer-type ligand architecture of four N-heterocyclic carbenes and two cyclometalating aryl units is presented. In this design the desired Fe(IV) is achieved while four low lying π* orbitals are easily available, thereby enabling a high energy MLCT excited state above the ground state which does not mix with the LMCT state located below the ground state. Transient absorption and streak camera measurements revealed a MLCT excited state lifetime of 4.6 ns, which is by far the longest MLCT state lifetime that is reported for iron species so far. The high energy of the MLCT excited state did not impede redox activity toward reductive and oxidative quenching. This study not only provides a further basis for photophysical behavior of first row transition metals, but also presents a new molecular design that allows a straightforward way to designing iron based photoredox catalysts and functional optical materials.

document.output:
Designing iron-based complexes as a sustainable alternative to rare-metal photosensitizers remains a formidable task due to the fast radiative decay of the charge-transfer states of these systems. A new molecular design based on a pincer-type ligand architecture of four N-heterocyclic carbenes and two cyclometalating aryl units is presented. In this design the desired Fe(IV) is achieved while four low lying π* orbitals are easily available, thereby enabling a high energy MLCT excited state above the ground state which does not mix with the LMCT state located below the ground state. document.input:
Developing iron-based complexes as sustainable alternatives to rare-metal photosensitizers is a major challenge due to the rapid non-radiative deactivation of their charge-transfer states.
